chunk_summary: Guidelines for balancing vitamin A, zinc, and copper in therapeutic feeds for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) emphasize protocol adherence, avoiding toxicity, and addressing specific deficiencies like xerophthalmia or measles.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vitamin A, zinc, and copper in SAM therapeutic feeds.
Identify when high-dose vitamin A supplementation is indicated in SAM care.
Recognize the risks of excessive zinc supplementation and its impact on copper balance.
Apply protocol-based micronutrient supplementation to avoid toxicity and ensure recovery.
key_facts: Routine high-dose vitamin A is unnecessary if therapeutic feeds already provide adequate amounts, except in cases like xerophthalmia or measles.
Excessive zinc supplementation without copper can lead to copper deficiency; protocols typically balance both.
Therapeutic feeds include standardized amounts of vitamin A and zinc, reducing the need for additional supplementation.
High-dose vitamin A is indicated for xerophthalmia, measles, or high-risk vitamin A deficiency when therapeutic feeds are unavailable.
Recovery outcomes include improved appetite, weight gain, and reduced infections over weeks.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Routine additional massive-dose vitamin A is not always required during rehabilitation if the child is receiving these therapeutic foods and has no eye signs; unnecessary extra dosing increases toxicity risk. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Zinc and copper balance in therapeutic feeds
